core_claim: AI makes phishing cheaper to personalize at scale, weakening 'spot the typo' awareness training.

slides.on_slide_copy: AI PHISHING MADE OLD TRAINING OBSOLETE
slides.subline: The scam is more personal now.
slides.visual_direction: Dark SOC scene, analyst, blurred inbox, faint synthetic face reflection; cyan glow; protected dark lower-third.
slides.visual_prompt: realistic editorial cybersecurity cover, a dark quiet security operations room with a single glowing sealed envelope resting on a desk, subtle single accent glow, strong negative space
slides.notes: Reuses the text-free pipeline cover background; React renders the headline on top.

slides.on_slide_copy: Old red flags like bad grammar and weird phrasing are fading.
slides.subline: The obvious tells were never the real defense.
slides.visual_direction: Abstract email previews with grammar-error marks dissolving; cyan accent; no readable text.
slides.visual_prompt: A single crisp clean paper letter rising out of a swirling cloud of torn crumpled paper scraps in the dark, the clean letter sharp and whole while the ragged scraps dissolve away, cinematic depth, lots of empty calm space in the lower half

slides.on_slide_copy: NCSC: AI gives attackers uplift in recon and social engineering.
slides.visual_direction: Public-profile fragments connecting toward a single inbox; cyan; no real names.
slides.visual_prompt: Faint anonymous human profile silhouettes and small floating shards of broken data drifting along thin trails of light toward one glowing sealed envelope in the dark, the bright envelope the clear focal point high in the frame, calm empty space below

slides.on_slide_copy: Not magic hacking. Cheaper personalization at scale.
slides.subline: AI lowers the cost, not the skill ceiling.
slides.visual_direction: Branching personalized message bubbles from an abstract workstation; safe, abstract only.
slides.visual_prompt: One glowing sealed envelope at the center multiplying outward into a wide sweeping fan of many identical paper envelopes flying off into the dark, a sense of endless effortless duplication at massive scale, focal in the upper area, empty space in the lower half

slides.on_slide_copy: We trained people to spot typos, not to verify requests.
slides.subline: Vocabulary checks don't stop clean messages.
slides.visual_direction: Employee near an approve button; ignored verification checklist behind.
slides.visual_prompt: A single lone human hand silhouette slowly reaching out to touch one glowing sealed envelope floating alone in the dark, quiet dangerous stillness, soft rim light on the hand and envelope, calm empty space in the lower third

slides.on_slide_copy: Verify by a second channel. Lock down resets and payment changes.
slides.visual_direction: Phone callback, ticket workflow, approval chain, identity-check icons.
slides.visual_prompt: Two vintage telephone handsets facing each other across the dark, joined by one bright steady glowing thread of light, a small closed padlock floating in the gap between them, calm orderly balanced composition, empty space in the lower third

slides.on_slide_copy: If your defense relies on scams {{looking fake}}, [[the game changed]].
slides.visual_direction: Analyst silhouette, lock icon, dark gradient; minimal copy.
slides.visual_prompt: A cracked and fractured magnifying glass in the dark, its broken lens revealing one perfectly smooth flawless sealed envelope behind the shattered glass, fine fracture lines spreading toward the edges, the center kept calm, focal weight toward the corners and edges

slides.on_slide_copy: Does your awareness training still teach “spot the typo”?
slides.subline: Save this for your next awareness review.
slides.visual_direction: Clean dark end card with subtle credibility cue and handle.
slides.visual_prompt: A single elegant magnifying glass hovering over one smooth blank sealed envelope in the dark, a soft glowing question mark shape floating near the upper edge, generous empty space, minimal and clean

caption:
AI phishing made “spot the typo” training obsolete.

The old red flags (bad grammar, clumsy phrasing, obvious urgency) were never the real defense. They were just the easiest tells. The UK's NCSC assessments describe AI giving attackers an uplift in reconnaissance and social engineering, which mostly means cheaper, more personalized lures at scale, not magical hacking.

For defenders, that's a process problem, not a vocabulary problem. If your training teaches people to hunt for spelling mistakes, a clean AI-written message walks right past it.

Defender move: verify sensitive requests (password resets, payment changes) over a second channel, and make that verification mandatory, not optional politeness.

Does your awareness training still lean on “spot the typo”?

Follow for AI security breakdowns without the fake panic.
comment_prompt: Does your awareness training still lean on “spot the typo”?
alt_text: Slide 1: Dark cinematic cover with bold headline 'AI phishing made old training obsolete' over a security operations scene with a cyan glow.
Slide 2: Context slide stating that old phishing red flags like bad grammar and weird phrasing are fading.
Slide 3: Risk slide noting the NCSC assessment that AI gives attackers an uplift in reconnaissance and social engineering.
Slide 4: Mechanism slide explaining the change is cheaper personalization at scale, not magic hacking.
Slide 5: Failure-point slide saying teams trained people to spot typos instead of verifying requests.
Slide 6: Defense slide recommending verification by a second channel and locking down resets and payment changes.
Slide 7: Takeaway slide: if your defense relies on scams looking fake, the game changed.
Slide 8: Call-to-action slide asking whether your awareness training still teaches 'spot the typo', with a save and comment prompt.

sources.source: NCSC — The near-term impact of AI on the cyber threat (2024)
sources.supports: AI provides capability uplift in reconnaissance and social engineering; fewer spelling/grammar tells in phishing.

sources.source: NCSC — The impact of AI on the cyber threat from now to 2027 (2025)
sources.supports: AI continues to make social engineering more effective and efficient through evolved TTPs.

narration.text: AI phishing made old training obsolete.
The old red flags, bad grammar and weird phrasing, are fading fast.
Verify sensitive requests over a second channel.
If your defense relies on scams looking fake, the game changed.
Does your training still teach people to spot the typo?
